ui.welcomeMessage:
Welcome to Linux Learning System!
Type your first command to begin learning.
ui.tabComplete: Press Tab to see available commands for this step
ui.resetConfirm: Are you sure you want to reset all progress?
ui.sandboxWelcome: Try any command — the sandbox is yours to explore.

lessons.title: Navigating the Filesystem
lessons.description: Learn how to move around the Linux filesystem using pwd, ls, and cd.

steps.description: The pwd command prints the full path of your current directory. It stands for 'Print Working Directory'.
steps.summary: You learned that pwd shows you where you are in the filesystem.

steps.instruction: List files in the current directory
steps.description: The ls command lists files and directories in your current location.
steps.summary: You learned that ls shows the contents of a directory.

steps.instruction: List files with details (hidden files included)
steps.description: Adding -la flags shows all files (including hidden ones) with permissions, size, and modification dates.
steps.summary: The ls -la combination gives a detailed view including hidden files.

steps.description: The cd command changes your current directory. /tmp is a temporary directory on Linux.
steps.summary: You navigated to /tmp using cd /tmp.

steps.description: Using cd ~ or just cd returns you to your home directory.
steps.summary: The ~ shortcut always refers to your home directory.

steps.description: The .. special directory refers to the parent directory.
steps.summary: You learned that cd .. moves you up one directory level.

lessons.title: Working with Files & Directories
lessons.description: Create, copy, move, and delete files and directories.

steps.description: The touch command creates an empty file or updates the timestamp of an existing one.
steps.summary: You created a file using touch hello.txt.

steps.description: The mkdir command creates a new directory.
steps.summary: You created a directory with mkdir myfolder.

steps.description: The cp command copies files from source to destination.
steps.summary: You copied a file using cp hello.txt hello_copy.txt.

steps.description: The mv command moves or renames files.
steps.summary: You renamed a file using mv.

steps.description: The rm command deletes files. Be careful — there is no trash bin in the terminal!
steps.summary: You deleted a file with rm. Always double-check before deleting!

lessons.title: Viewing File Contents
lessons.description: Learn how to view and inspect file contents using cat, head, tail, and wc.

steps.instruction: Display the contents of a file
steps.description: The cat command (concatenate) displays the entire content of a file.
steps.summary: You used cat to display a file's contents.

steps.instruction: Show the first 10 lines of a file
steps.description: The head command shows the beginning of a file. By default it shows 10 lines.
steps.summary: You used head to view the first lines of a file.

steps.instruction: Show the last 10 lines of a file
steps.description: The tail command shows the end of a file. Useful for logs!
steps.summary: You used tail to see the last lines of a file.

steps.instruction: Count lines, words, and characters in a file
steps.description: The wc command (word count) shows line, word, and character counts.
steps.summary: The wc command counts lines, words, and characters in files.

lessons.title: File Permissions
lessons.description: Understand and modify file permissions with chmod and chown.

steps.instruction: Add execute permission to a file
steps.description: The chmod command changes file permissions. +x adds execute permission.
steps.summary: You added execute permission with chmod +x.

steps.description: Numeric mode: 7=rwx, 5=r-x, 5=r-x. Owner gets full access, others read/execute.
steps.summary: Numeric permissions: chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x.

steps.instruction: Change the owner of a file
steps.description: The chown command changes file ownership. Format: chown owner:group file.
steps.summary: You changed file ownership with chown.

lessons.title: Searching & Text Processing
lessons.description: Search and process text using grep, sort, and cut.

steps.instruction: Search for a pattern in a file
steps.description: The grep command searches for patterns in files. It's one of the most powerful Linux commands.
steps.summary: You used grep to search for lines containing 'root'.

steps.description: The -i flag makes the search case-insensitive.
steps.summary: The -i flag makes grep ignore case.

steps.description: The sort command sorts lines in a file alphabetically.
steps.summary: You used sort to alphabetically sort file contents.

steps.instruction: Cut specific fields from each line
steps.description: The cut command extracts columns or fields from text. Here we extract usernames using : delimiter.
steps.summary: You extracted the first field (username) using cut -d: -f1.

lessons.title: Process Management
lessons.description: Manage running processes with ps, top, and kill.

steps.description: The ps command shows a snapshot of current processes.
steps.expectedOutput:
PID TTY          TIME CMD
 1234 pts/0    00:00:00 bash
 5678 pts/0    00:00:00 ps
steps.summary: The ps command lists your running processes.

steps.description: The ps aux combination shows all processes from all users with detailed information.
steps.expectedOutput:
USER       PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root         1  0.0  0.5  98765  1234 ?        Ss   ...
steps.summary: You used ps aux to view all system processes.

steps.description: The kill command sends a signal to a process. SIGTERM (15) asks it to terminate gracefully.
steps.summary: You sent a termination signal with kill.

lessons.title: Networking Basics
lessons.description: Learn basic networking commands: ping, curl, and ss.

steps.description: The ping command sends ICMP echo requests to test if a host is reachable.
steps.expectedOutput:
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: icmp_seq=1 ttl=118 time=12.3 ms
...
steps.summary: You used ping -c 4 to send 4 ICMP packets to Google's DNS.

steps.instruction: Fetch a URL from the command line
steps.description: The curl command transfers data to/from servers using various protocols.
steps.summary: You fetched a webpage using curl.

steps.description: The ss command shows network socket information. It's the modern replacement for netstat.
steps.expectedOutput:
State      Recv-Q Send-Q  Local Address:Port  Peer Address:Port
LISTEN     0      128     0.0.0.0:22          0.0.0.0:*
steps.summary: You viewed listening TCP sockets with ss -tln.

lessons.title: Archives & Compression
lessons.description: Create and extract archives using tar, gzip, and zip.

steps.description: The tar command creates archives. -czf = create, gzip, file.
steps.summary: You created a compressed archive with tar -czf.

steps.description: The -xzf flags extract a gzip-compressed tar archive.
steps.summary: You extracted an archive with tar -xzf.

steps.description: The zip command creates a ZIP archive. -r makes it recursive (include subdirectories).
steps.summary: You created a ZIP archive with zip -r.

steps.description: The unzip command extracts ZIP archives.
steps.summary: You extracted a ZIP archive with unzip.

lessons.title: User & Group Management
lessons.description: Manage users and groups with whoami, id, useradd, and passwd.

steps.description: The whoami command simply prints your current username.
steps.summary: You identified yourself with whoami.

steps.description: The id command shows user ID (UID), group ID (GID), and all groups you belong to.
steps.summary: You viewed your user identity with id.

lessons.title: Shell Scripting Basics
lessons.description: Get started with shell scripting: echo, variables, and loops.

steps.description: The echo command prints text to the terminal. It's essential for scripting.
steps.summary: You printed text using echo.

steps.description: In bash, variables are assigned with = (no spaces) and referenced with $.
steps.summary: You created a variable NAME and referenced it with $NAME.

steps.description: A for loop iterates over a list. The syntax: for var in list; do ...; done.
steps.command: for i in 1 2 3; do echo $i; done
steps.summary: You ran a for loop that printed numbers 1 through 3.
